fields.Front: Nice try — I'm not buying that excuse.
fields.Back:
A mamá mona con bananas verdes.
Ej: “¿Me vas a decir que llegaste tarde por el tráfico? A mamá mona con bananas verdes.”
fields.Interesting Facts:
Origen: The image is of trying to fool a mama monkey with unripe (green) bananas — she knows better. Equivalent to “Tell it to the marines” or “Nice try — I’m not buying it.”

Variante similar: “A otro perro con ese hueso.” (Try that trick on someone else.)

Expresión para decirle a alguien que no intente engañarnos o mentirnos
fields.user_feedback: Nice example, but the text on the front does not make it possible to deduce even though the phrase itself I'm I'm producing quite well

fields.Front: to die of thirst
fields.Back: morir de sed
fields.Interesting Facts: *morir de* + noun/emotion — *morir de hambre, de frío, de vergüenza, de risa*. Colloquially: *me muero de sed*.
fields.user_feedback: Front: [remake the card into a natural sentence. Sth like I'm dying from hunger as we speak, up for a burger? In the interesting facts. You can also add other potential exaggerated causes of death. Hahaha like a dying of thirst and dying of boredom]

fields.Front: Nevertheless / However (starting a contrasting sentence)
fields.Back: Sin embargo, …
fields.Interesting Facts: Marks sharp contrast. Slightly more formal than *pero*; common in narrative and clear oral speech.
fields.user_feedback: [consider adding small or putting it into a small phrase. A philosophical one from Plato's alcibiades for example]

fields.Front: we set off / we got moving
fields.Back: nos pusimos en marcha
fields.Interesting Facts: *ponerse en marcha* = to set off. Also figurative: *el proyecto se puso en marcha*. Argentine colloquial: *arrancar*.
fields.user_feedback: Hint: [The hint is too verbous. I think either of the words standalone would be enough]

fields.Front: He wants a raise, fewer hours, AND more vacation — he wants everything.
fields.Back:
Querer la chancha y los veinte (y la máquina de hacer chorizos)
Ej: “Quiere aumento, menos horas y más vacaciones — quiere la chancha y los veinte.”
fields.Interesting Facts:
¿Por qué están los paréntesis?
La parte entre paréntesis — y la máquina de hacer chorizos — es una extensión humorística de la frase base. La frase original y más usada es simplemente “querer la chancha y los veinte”; lo de la máquina es un remate cómico que se agrega para exagerar aún más la avaricia — opcional, tiene efecto de chiste.

Origen: Literalmente querer la chancha (cerda), los veinte (lechones o pesos) y hasta la máquina para hacer embutidos — el animal, su prole y el equipo. Imagen de codicia total, arraigada en la cultura rioplatense del campo y el almacén.

Querer obtener todos los beneficios sin ceder en nada o quererlo todo
fields.user_feedback: I don't understand the point of this hint it seems it's quite useless

fields.Front: You'll feel like laughing with me
fields.Back: Sentirás ganas de reír conmigo
fields.Interesting Facts: *Tener/sentir ganas de* + infinitive = to feel like doing something. More literary than *vas a tener ganas de*.
fields.user_feedback: In on the back show the example also or append the example of how it can be used naturally in conversation because now I cannot imagine

fields.Front: Not colod, not hot, windy & rains a bit
fields.Back:
No hace frío, no hace calor, hace viento y llueve un poco.
Hace sol y no llueve.
fields.user_feedback: Edit an interesting fact or add an interesting fact about whether in Rioplatense Spanish it will be the same

fields.Front: The Sagrada Familia is over there.
fields.Back:
La Sagrada Familia queda por allá.
El museo queda cerca.

Por allá emphasizes direction/general area ("over that way"), while bare allá simply points to a location. Both are correct; por allá feels more natural when gesturing toward a zone rather than a precise spot.
fields.user_feedback: Move what shall be in interesting facts to interesting facts

fields.Front: I remember that it was a leap in my understanding.
fields.Back:
Me acuerdo de que fue un salto en mi comprensión.
Me acuerdo de ese momento.
fields.user_feedback: At an interesting fact on what is the difference versus 'yo acuerdo' If it is even grammatically correct?

fields.Front: Comer un poco para eliminar la sensación de hambre
fields.Back:
Quitarse el hambre — to quell hunger.
— Comí un alfajor para quitarme el hambre antes de la cena.
fields.Interesting Facts:
picar algo — to snack / nibble.
— Llegó dos horas antes; pasó por la cocina a picar algo.

engañar al estómago — to trick one’s stomach (eat just enough to ease it).
— Tomate una galletita así engañás al estómago hasta que esté lista la comida.
fields.user_feedback: Hint is useless

fields.Front: The edge of the pool is very wet. Suddenly, he falls.
fields.Back: El borde de la pileta está muy mojado. De repente, se cae.
fields.Interesting Facts: In Argentina, “pileta” is more common than “piscina”.
fields.user_feedback: Why está and not este?

fields.Front: He's a smooth-talking liar — total con man.
fields.Back:
Ser un gato / Ser alto gato
Ej: “Ese tipo es un gato, no le creas nada.” / “Esa es alto gato.”
fields.Interesting Facts:
Persona mujeriega, mentirosa o “chanta” (si es hombre) o prostituta VIP (si es mujer)

¿Por qué “alto” y no “alta”? En el argot rioplatense, alto/a como intensificador puede quedar invariable cuando precede al sustantivo base — “alto gato” es la forma fija del compuesto; algunos hablantes dicen “alta gata” pero “alto gato” funciona para ambos géneros en registro informal.
fields.user_feedback: The part where on the back it says you don't have to trust him. He's not possible to retrieve from the front taken to consideration that I sometimes am seeing these cards of the six or even more months

fields.Front: "Meh." / "So-so." / "Nothing special." (lukewarm reaction)
fields.Back:
Ni muy muy, ni tan tan.

Lit. "neither very very, nor so so." Fixed expression for an underwhelming or middling result.
fields.user_feedback: Is it also used in Rioplatense Spanish?

fields.Front: I'm going to set the trap to catch the opossum that comes to kill the chickens and steal the eggs
fields.Back: Voy a poner la trampa para cazar a la comadreja que viene a matar los pollos y robar los huevos
fields.Interesting Facts: "voy a + infinitive" = near future. comadreja = weasel-like small predator in Rioplatense rural speech.
fields.user_feedback: The interesting fact about 'voy a' Is a pointless because I am too much advanced for it already

fields.Front: After two weeks, the little coati was roaming free and would go by himself at night to his cage
fields.Back: Al cabo de quince días, el coaticito andaba suelto y él mismo se iba de noche a su jaula
fields.Hint: al cabo de / andaba suelto / se iba
fields.Interesting Facts: "al cabo de" = after (a period of time). "andaba suelto" = imperfect of andar + adjective, ongoing state. "él mismo" = himself (emphasis). "se iba" = habitual past.
fields.user_feedback: 15 days and 2 weeks are not the same thing. The back is correct but front has to be edited

fields.Text: {{c1::Al cabo de::after}} quince días, el coaticito andaba suelto
fields.Back Extra: al cabo de + time period = after / at the end of. Literary/formal connector in written Argentine Spanish.
fields.user_feedback: He provide an interesting fact on the spoken alternative in day-to-day Argentine Spanish

fields.Front: When someone looks like they just bit into a lemon — all sour-faced and grumpy
fields.Back: Estás hecho un limón.
fields.Interesting Facts: "Estar hecho un limón" describes a sour, pinched, unhappy expression — like the face you make biting a lemon. Follows the pattern "estar hecho un/una + [noun]," e.g. "estás hecho un desastre" (you're a mess).
fields.user_feedback: Two interesting facts. Also add whether it is frequently used phrase or no. Maybe a few example sentences and maybe if there is something more popular instead of a lemon point that out as well
